corrections.text: The article attributes the Amaterasu particle's energy of "244 EeV" to the arXiv preprint (2405.17409). The cited arXiv page is the abstract, which states only that the highest-energy cosmic rays reach "beyond ~100 EeV" and that energy-loss lengths apply at "≲300 EeV"; it does not give the 244 EeV figure. The 244 EeV value is accurate and is reported by Tech Times ("about 244 exa-electron volts"); Penn State's release gives "about 240 exa-electron volts." The correct citation for the specific figure is Tech Times, not the arXiv abstract.

The article states that the study "examined specific heavy elements as candidates — among them selenium, tellurium, and platinum, extending up to uranium," attributed to the arXiv paper. None of the four named elements (selenium, tellurium, platinum, uranium) appear in any of the five cited sources, including the captured arXiv abstract page. The general claim that the candidates are nuclei heavier than the iron group is supported by Penn State and ScienceDaily; the specific element names are not traceable to a cited source and should be treated as unverified.
